summary: Bryan Cantrill recaps dtrace.conf(08), an unconference that brought together 70 software engineers working across the entire technology stack, from FPGAs to high-level languages like Erlang. The conference featured live demos on production machines and Mac laptops, showcasing DTrace's ability to bridge different layers of the software stack. Cantrill emphasizes the unique cross-pollination of talent at the event, where dinner conversations naturally mixed people from different companies and technology domains.
key_points: DTrace cuts across vertical boundaries in the software stack, from PHP through to the kernel
Conference covered everything from FPGAs to VMs to operating systems to high-level languages
Nearly every presentation included a live demo, many on production machines or Mac laptops
The Leopard port opened new vistas for DTrace on a popular development platform
Open sourcing DTrace has been in Sun's best business interest
Cross-domain mixing at dinner showed the unique community-building power of the event
money_quotes: DTrace is able to cut across vertical boundaries in the software stack (taking you from say, PHP through to the kernel).

As it is the nature of DTrace to reflect not the flowery theory of a system but rather its brutish reality, it was not at all surprising (but refreshing nonetheless) that nearly every presentation had a demo to go with it.

I claimed that the room had as much software talent as has ever been assembled in 70 people; after seeing the day's presentations and the many discussions that they spawned, I would double down on that claim in an instant.

It almost brought a tear to the eye to see such interaction among such superlative software talent from such disjoint domains, and my dinner partner captured the zeitgeist precisely: 'it's like a commune in here.'
key_insight: The greatest value of a technology community conference lies not just in the technical content but in the cross-pollination that occurs when talented people from different layers of the stack come together around a unifying tool.
